revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Inau]][[fr:Inau]][[ha:Inau]][[ja:イナウ]][[ko:이나우]][[pl:Inau]][[ru:Инау]]
{{Short description|Ainu ritual wood-shaving stick}} {{Other uses|Inău (disambiguation){{!}}Inău}}
'''Inau''' or '''Inaw''' ({{ill|Ainu language|en|Ainu language|qid=Q27969|lt=Ainu|1=Ainu language}}: イナウ or イナゥ) is an {{ill|Ainu language|en|Ainu language|qid=Q27969|lt=Ainu|1=Ainu language}} term for a ritual wood-shaving stick used in Ainu prayers to the spiritual world. They were used in most [[Ainu people#Religion|Ainu religious rituals]], and were also frequently made to request assistance for hunting and childbirth. Some can be used multiple times, while others are destroyed immediately after one use. Their size and the direction in which they are shaved depends on which [[kamuy]] it is offered to and what is being requested.
==Etymology== {{Contains special characters|Manchu}} The word ''inau'' appears to be a {{ill|en:Loanword|en|en:Loanword|qid=Q103808|lt=en:Loanword|1=Loanword}} from other languages in the region, probably the [[:en:Orok language|Orok language]] of {{ill|Sakhalin|en|Sakhalin|qid=Q7792|lt=Sakhalin|1=Sakhalin}} (''cf.'' Orok ''illau'' < *''ilawun''). It is most likely ultimately related to {{ill|Manchu language|en|Manchu language|qid=Q33638|lt=Manchu|1=Manchu language}} ''ila-''/{{ManchuSibeUnicode|ᡳᠯᠠᠮᠪᡳ}}(ila-mbi) "to blossom" and ''ilha''/{{ManchuSibeUnicode|ᡳᠯᡥᠠ}} "flower."<ref>{{cite journal|last=Ikegami |first=Jiro |url=http://ci.nii.ac.jp/naid/110001101136/en |title=Remarks on the Origin of the Ainu Word inaw : with Reference to the Etymology of the Uilta Word illau |journal=Japanese Journal of Ethnology |via=Ci.nii.ac.jp |date=1980-03-31 |access-date=2011-03-12 |volume=44 |number=4 |pages=393–402 |doi=10.14890/minkennewseries.44.4_393 |language=ja}}</ref> The [[:en:Nivkh language|Nivkh]] word ''inau'' ~ ''nau'' is most likely a loanword from the same Manchu-Tungus source via Ainu; both Tungusic languages and Nivkh have an {{IPA|/l/}} sound, which the Ainu language lacks, so the replacement of {{IPA|/l/}} with {{IPA|/n/}} is probably an artifact of Ainu phonology.<ref name = "Tsumagari2009">Tsumagari, Toshirō, 「サハリンの言語世界 : 単語借用から見る」 ''saharin no gengo sekai: tango shakuyō kara miru'' ("The Linguistic World of Sakhalin: A View from Loanwords"), Hokkaido University Collection of Scholarly and Academic Papers (2009).</ref>
==Description== [[Image:Inaw.jpg|thumb|right|200px|Inau sticks]] To make inau, the bark of a branch is first peeled and then a knife called an "inawke-makiri" is repeatedly used to shave the wood into thin curled strips that form a tuft.<ref>{{cite web |url=http://www.city.sapporo.jp/shimin/pirka-kotan/en/kogei/inaw/index.html |title=Sapporo Ainu Culture Promotion Center (Sapporo Pirka Kotan) |publisher=City of Sapporo |date=2011-02-23 |access-date=2011-03-12 |archive-date=2012-09-26 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20120926020156/http://www.city.sapporo.jp/shimin/pirka-kotan/en/kogei/inaw/index.html |url-status=dead }}</ref> Inau are usually anywhere from {{convert|5|in|cm|spell=in}} to {{convert|1|ft|cm|spell=in}} in length. There are several different types of inau, all with different variations in length and thickness depending on purpose. Certain types of wood were also used depending on the inau's purpose. {{ill|Willow|en|Willow|qid=Q36050|lt=Willow|1=Willow}} was exclusively used for offerings to good kamuy, and is preferred for this purpose, although a few other woods could be used if necessary. These inau tend to be close to a foot long. Conversely, inau made to repel illnesses and evil spirits (called ''wen inau'') were typically made from {{ill|Alder|en|Alder|qid=Q25239|lt=Alder|1=Alder}}, and are usually around {{convert|8|in|cm|spell=in}} long. Some inau also have wings attached to them, although it is unclear exactly what function they served. The number of wings varies by region; Ainu from southern [[:en:Hokkaidō|Hokkaidō]] typically made them with nine wings, while further north they were made with six or seven.<ref name="Ainu Creed and Cult">{{cite book|title=Ainu Creed and Cult |publisher=Columbia University Press |last=Munro |first=Neil Gordon |year=1963}}</ref> Inau also have shavings attached to them. Most point upwards, although ''chiahorokakepe'' or ''chehorokakep'' (Ainu チアホロカケペ or チェホロカケㇷ゚, literally "the thing shaven backward") have them pointed downward, making them very distinct from other inau. In addition, certain kinds of inau, such as [[Chikube-ni]] inau, are equipped with spears.<ref name=Inau>{{cite web|url=http://www.fmnh.org/research_collections/anthropology/anthro_sites/boone/ainu/ainu_com/ainu_com8.html |title=Inau |publisher=The Field Museum |access-date=July 18, 2011 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20110927105319/http://www.fmnh.org/research_collections/anthropology/anthro_sites/boone/ainu/ainu_com/ainu_com8.html |archive-date=September 27, 2011 |url-status=dead}}</ref>
==Uses== With the exception of funerals, for which they were never made, inau were an important part of rituals involving kamuy. Because only men were permitted to participate in religious rituals besides the bear ceremony, only men could make inau. Before building a house, four inau and one chiahorokakepe were placed where the hearth was to be built. Every Ainu house had inau on the hearth in honor of [[:en:Kamuy Fuchi|Kamuy Fuchi]] (known in Sakhalin Ainu as Unci Kamuy), and during Ainu house-warming ceremonies, three to four additional chiahorokakepe were typically placed on the hearth. Inau were also made to requests assistance from progressively more powerful kamuy during exorcism rituals and difficult childbirth.<ref name="Ainu Creed and Cult"/><ref name="Iku-Nishi of the Saghalein Ainu">{{cite journal|jstor=331115 |title=Iku-Nishi of the Saghalein Ainu |publisher=Royal Anthropological Institute |journal=Journal of the Royal Anthropological Institute |year=1949 |volume=2 |pages=107–117}}</ref>
In addition to personal use, inau were also used in larger ceremonies. Ainu bear ceremonies featured a large number of inau, of all different kinds. Certain elders, known as ''ekashi'', would also use inau in ceremonies to control the weather. Finally, inau were used by hunters both before and during a hunt to bring good luck.<ref name="Ainu Creed and Cult"/>
Inau made for curing diseases were dismantled immediately after their purpose was served. Keeping them for any longer than was absolutely necessary was considered extremely dangerous, and it was thought they could cause a great deal of harm to the person who made them. Conversely, inau made to more benevolent kamuy were not so quickly destroyed.<ref name="Ainu Creed and Cult"/>
==See also== * {{ill|Chichilaki|en|Chichilaki|qid=Q1754111|lt=Chichilaki|1=Chichilaki}} * {{ill|Ikupasuy|en|Ikupasuy|qid=Q4391537|lt=Ikupasuy|1=Ikupasuy}} * {{ill|Flail|en|Flail|qid=Q1427748|lt=Flail|1=Flail}} * [[Glossary of Shinto]] * ''[[Gohei]]'' – wooden wands used in Shinto rituals * {{transliteration|ja|{{ill|Harae|en|Harae|qid=Q3127244|lt=Harae|1=Harae}}}} * {{transliteration|ja|{{ill|Hu (ritual baton)|en|Hu (ritual baton)|qid=Q1185997|lt=Hu|1=Hu (ritual baton)}}}} * {{transliteration|ja|{{ill|Gunbai|en|Gunbai|qid=Q1188570|lt=Gunbai|1=Gunbai}}}} * ''{{ill|Ōnusa|en|Ōnusa|qid=Q1153887|lt=Ōnusa|1=Ōnusa}}'' – [[Shinto]] version of the ''inau'' * {{transliteration|ja|{{ill|Saihai|en|Saihai|qid=Q7400093|lt=Saihai|1=Saihai}}}} * {{ill|Ruyi (scepter)|en|Ruyi (scepter)|qid=Q1448880|lt=Ruyi (scepter)|1=Ruyi (scepter)}}
